desc: listen and respond
use language conventions and non-verbal communication
understand and use new words and develop morphological awareness
name upper- and lowercase letters (both in and out of order)
use proper formation patterns to write letters
isolate and blend phonemes, and segment simple words into phonemes
identify simple grapheme-phoneme correspondences
read and spell simple words using phonemic awareness and phonics knowledge
read short sentences fluently, including those with explicitly taught words, with increasing accuracy, to support comprehension
identify purposes for choosing different texts
apply knowledge of words, sentence structure, and punctuation
read and understand simple texts, using background knowledge and beginning knowledge of words, grammar, and sentence structure
express personal thoughts and feelings about texts
make predictions about unfamiliar texts, using background knowledge
make connections to a variety of texts
use knowledge of letters, words, and sentences, with pictures and symbols, to create simple texts
use classroom writing resources to support creating simple texts
explore what numbers are used for
understand when counting forward, the quantity gets bigger and when counting backward, the quantity gets smaller
identify and write numbers to 20
subitize quantities to 5
compose and decompose quantities to 10
understand the concept of more than, the same (equal), or less than
estimate the number of objects in small collections and check by counting
count to 20 by 1s, starting from any number
solve equal-share problems using concrete materials
describe relationships between any two numbers from 0 to 20
recall addition facts to 5 and related subtraction facts
solve situations involving addition and subtraction to 10 using concrete materials
identify and explore the value of Canadian coins to 25¢ and coins and bills to $10
identify, describe, and create patterns
identify the core of a pattern and describe why it is important
show that patterns can be represented in different ways because the core remains the same
extend patterns in both directions and identify any missing elements
sort objects or data using one attribute
collect and record data to answer questions of interest, and display it in concrete graphs or picture graphs
respond to data in concrete graphs and picture graphs by interpreting it and drawing conclusions
describe how likely familiar events are to happen
identify, sort, and compare the most common two-dimensional shapes, including circle, hexagon, rhombus, and trapezoid
identify, sort, and compare the most common three-dimensional objects, including cube, sphere, prisms, cone, and pyramid
in structures they have made, identify three-dimensional objects and two-dimensional shapes
construct two-dimensional pictures and three-dimensional structures with matching halves
put two-dimensional shapes together to make other shapes, break them into smaller shapes, and put them together in different ways
describe basic spatial relationships and movements, and visualize objects from different perspectives
give and follow directions for moving from one location to another
describe different ways that objects can be measured
compare the length, area, mass, and capacity of objects using concrete materials
use directional and positional language to create and follow instructions involving movement
test and refine instructions
communicate and record step-by-step instructions using symbols, words, or pictures
state problems and pose questions
make predictions and observations while exploring, investigating, and designing
select materials and tools to carry out investigations and to construct models and structures
draw conclusions and communicate results
design, build, and test models and structures
follow safety rules to help prevent injuries, including concussion, and demonstrate an understanding of the importance of these practices
ask questions about and describe some natural occurrences
sort and classify groups of living and non-living things
compare patterns in natural and built environments
act and talk with peers and adults by expressing and accepting positive messages
show the ability to take turns
demonstrate an awareness of ways of making and keeping friends
use problem-solving skills in social situations
demonstrate self-reliance and a sense of responsibility
demonstrate a willingness to try new experiences and to adapt to new situations
demonstrate self-motivation, initiative, and confidence in their approach to learning
demonstrate self-control and adapt behaviour to different contexts
develop empathy for others, and acknowledge and respond to each other’s feelings
demonstrate an understanding of the effects of healthy, active living on the mind and body
investigate the benefits of nutritious foods and explore ways of ensuring healthy eating
practise and discuss appropriate personal hygiene that promotes personal, family, and community health
discuss what action to take when they feel unsafe or uncomfortable, in person or online, and when and how to seek assistance
discuss and demonstrate in play what makes them happy and unhappy, and why
participate actively in creative movement and other daily physical activities
demonstrate persistence while engaged in activities that require the use of both large and small muscles
demonstrate strategies for engaging in cooperative play in a variety of games and activities
demonstrate spatial awareness in activities that require the use of large muscles
demonstrate control of large muscles with and without equipment
demonstrate balance, whole-body and hand-eye coordination, and flexibility in movement
demonstrate control of small muscles while working in a variety of learning areas and when using a variety of materials or equipment
demonstrate spatial awareness by doing activities that require the use of small muscles
recognize personal interests, strengths, and accomplishments
identify and talk about their own interests and preferences
express their thoughts and share experiences
understand that everyone belongs to multiple groups and communities, and that these groups and communities together shape Canadian identity
describe, both verbally and non-verbally, ways in which they contribute to the various groups to which they belong
demonstrate respect for different perspectives and identify areas of connection
stand up for themselves, and apply behaviours that enhance their own and others’ well- being
think critically about fair/unfair and biased behaviour towards both themselves and others, and act with compassion and kindness
recognize discriminatory and inequitable practices and behaviours and respond appropriately
recognize people in their community and talk about what they do
recognize places and buildings within their community, both natural and human-made, and talk about their functions
identify similarities and differences between local environments
describe what would happen if something in the local environment changed
describe ways in which they care for and respect the environment
express their responses to dance and drama
respond to texts and art forms from various cultures and communities through classroom drama activities and make connections to their own experiences
express their responses to music by moving, by making connections to their own experiences, or by talking about the musical form
explore the elements of dance, drama, music, and design
experiment with different materials, techniques, and processes used in a variety of art forms
use problem-solving skills and imagination to create forms of art
communicate ideas through dance, drama, music, and visual arts
